content:
# Acts Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 238-239 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_011_page_001.jpg, ACT_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now the apostles and the brothers throughout Judea heard that the Gentiles too had received the word of God. **2** So when Peter went up to Jerusalem, those of the circumcision party challenged him, **3** saying, "You went into the homes of uncircumcised men and ate with them!"

**4** Peter began and laid the whole thing out for them, step by step:

**5** "I was in the city of Joppa, praying, when I fell into a trance and had a vision. I saw something like a great sheet being lowered from the sky by its four corners, and it came right down to where I was. **6** I looked into it carefully and saw four-footed animals of the earth, wild beasts, reptiles, and birds of the sky. **7** Then I heard a voice say to me, 'Get up, Peter. Kill and eat.'

**8** "But I said, 'Absolutely not, Lord! Nothing impure or unclean has ever entered my mouth.'

**9** "The voice from heaven answered a second time, 'What God has made clean, you must not call impure.' **10** This happened three times, and then the whole thing was pulled back up into heaven.

**11** "And right at that moment, three men who had been sent to me from Caesarea arrived at the house where I was staying. **12** The Spirit told me to go with them without hesitation. These six brothers also went with me, and we entered the man's house. **13** He told us how he had seen an angel stand in his house and say, 'Send to Joppa and bring back Simon who is called Peter. **14** He will bring you a message through which you and your entire household will be saved.'

**15** "As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell on them just as it had on us at the beginning. **16** Then I remembered what the Lord had said: 'John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit.' **17** So if God gave them the same gift he gave us when we believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to stand in God's way?"

**18** When they heard this, they had nothing more to say. They praised God and declared, "So then, God has given even the Gentiles the chance to turn to him and live!"

**19** Now those who had been scattered by the persecution that broke out over Stephen traveled as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, but they spoke the word to no one except Jews. **20** Some of them, however — men from Cyprus and Cyrene — went to Antioch and began speaking to Greeks as well, telling them the good news about the Lord Jesus. **21** The hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number believed and turned to the Lord.

**22** News of this reached the ears of the church in Jerusalem, and they sent Barnabas to Antioch. **23** When he arrived and saw the grace of God at work, he was overjoyed and urged them all to remain true to the Lord with unwavering hearts. **24** He was a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and faith, and a large crowd was brought to the Lord.

**25** Then Barnabas went to Tarsus to look for Saul, **26** and when he found him, he brought him to Antioch. For a whole year they met with the church and taught large numbers of people. And it was in Antioch that the disciples were first called Christians.

**27** During this time, prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch. **28** One of them, named Agabus, stood up and predicted through the Spirit that a severe famine would spread across the entire Roman world — which in fact happened under Claudius. **29** So the disciples, each according to their ability, decided to send relief to the brothers and sisters living in Judea. **30** They did this, sending their gift to the elders through Barnabas and Saul.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2-3 — "those of the circumcision party challenged him":** The Greek (οἱ ἐκ περιτομῆς διεκρίνοντο πρὸς αὐτόν) captures a factional identity — these were not simply circumcised Jewish believers, but a party within the early church that insisted on maintaining Jewish boundary markers. I render "challenged" rather than "criticized" or "contended" because the scene is one of confrontation demanding an accounting, not mere grumbling.

**Verse 4 — "laid the whole thing out for them, step by step":** The Greek (ἐξετίθετο αὐτοῖς καθεξῆς) literally means "set forth to them in order." The dynamic rendering captures Peter's deliberate, sequential narration — he is building his case methodically for a skeptical audience.

**Verse 12 — "without hesitation":** Sinaiticus reads μηδὲν διακρίναντα ("making no distinction" or "without doubting"). This is a key thematic echo — the same root (διακρίνω) used of the circumcision party's challenge in verse 2 is now the Spirit's command to Peter. The Spirit says: make no distinction. The old dividing walls are down.

**Verse 14 — "you and your entire household will be saved":** Sinaiticus includes σὺ καὶ πᾶς ὁ οἶκός σου, the full household salvation formula. This is significant — salvation here is communal, sweeping through a Roman household from paterfamilias to servants.

**Verse 18 — "the chance to turn to him and live":** The Greek (τὴν μετάνοιαν εἰς ζωήν) is literally "repentance unto life." I render dynamically to capture the astonishment — God has opened the door of life-giving transformation to the Gentiles. The emphasis falls on the breathtaking generosity of God.

**Verse 20 — "speaking to Greeks":** Sinaiticus reads Ἑλληνιστάς (Hellenists/Greek-speakers) rather than the variant Ἕλληνας (Greeks/Gentiles). The distinction matters: these may be Greek-speaking Jews initially, though the context clearly contrasts them with the "Jews only" mission of verse 19. The narrative logic demands that a new audience is being reached — hence I render "Greeks" to capture the force of the breakthrough.

**Verse 26 — "first called Christians":** The Greek χρηματίσαι Χριστιανούς marks a pivotal moment in identity. The verb χρηματίζω can mean "to be divinely called" or simply "to be publicly known as." Whether this was a label given by outsiders (perhaps mockingly — "the Christ-people") or a name embraced from within, Antioch is where the movement first gained a name distinct from Judaism.

**Verse 28 — "the entire Roman world":** The Greek (ἐφ᾽ ὅλην τὴν οἰκουμένην) literally means "upon the whole inhabited world," which in first-century usage meant the Roman Empire. I render "Roman world" to avoid the misleading impression of a truly global event while preserving the intended scope.

— *PNEUMA*
